role_summary: Civil Rights History Project interviewee; the LoC oral history is the primary archival source for this catalog page.

photo.alt: C. T. Vivian addressing the Association for the Study of African American Life and History convention in Atlanta, September 2015

ai_reading: Vivian's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17) at a high cosine count of 330, a notably strong single-neighbor signature in this catalog. The McLaurin pairing is the catalog's clearest example of a SCLC-and-Mississippi-SNCC cross-cohort cosine: McLaurin's Sunflower County voter-registration work and Vivian's SCLC field-secretary direct-action work overlapped in Mississippi in 1962-1964, and the embedding pulls their testimony together more strongly than either's pairing with their own organization's institutional headquarters. He appears in multiple SCLC catalog pages' top-five neighbor lists, suggesting the corpus reads him as a central SCLC voice the way it reads Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132) as the SCLC chief-of-staff voice. The novel observation his page surfaces is the field-secretary-vs-headquarters role distinction: Vivian's organizational title was National Director of Affiliates, which the cultural reception often glosses as a back-office logistical role; the embedding instead pulls his testimony toward the same cluster as the on-the-ground Mississippi organizers, suggesting the corpus catches the direct-action register of his Selma confrontation, his Birmingham work, and his post-1968 National Anti-Klan Network direct-action vocabulary over the headquarters-affiliates organizational frame.
biographical_paragraph: C. T. Vivian's Civil Rights History Project oral history is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript. His external biographical record is well-documented [src: 1]. Vivian was born July 28, 1924 in Boonville, Missouri, and grew up in Macomb, Illinois; he completed undergraduate work at Western Illinois University, then attended American Baptist College in Nashville beginning in 1955. He became one of the founding members of the SCLC and served as the organization's National Director of Affiliates from 1962, traveling the South to establish and coordinate local SCLC chapters [src: 2]. On February 16, 1965 on the Dallas County courthouse steps in Selma he confronted Sheriff Jim Clark over the denial of voter registration to Black citizens; Clark punched him in the mouth on camera, the photographed moment becoming one of the documented turning points of the campaign that produced the Voting Rights Act of 1965 [src: 2]. After King's April 4, 1968 assassination he founded the National Anti-Klan Network (later renamed the Center for Democratic Renewal) and continued direct-action work into the early twenty-first century [src: 2]. In 2013 President Barack Obama presented him the Presidential Medal of Freedom. He died July 17, 2020 in Atlanta, the same day as John Lewis, at ninety-five [src: 2]. His own 1970 theological volume Black Power and the American Myth (Fortress Press) is the catalog's primary scholarly source for his political-theological framing [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: C. T. Vivian
interview_snippets.lead_in: Vivian describes two distinct currents he saw running inside the freedom movement, and names what set the nonviolent strand in the United States apart from the Gandhian movement in India:
interview_snippets.quote: One of them was by any means necessary, and the other one was nonviolent direct action. And with a biblical base--difference, that's a real difference between nonviolence as we had known it in India, and what we knew here, we did it with a biblical base, based upon Jesus. But there were great deep religious undergirding for nonviolence in India as far as that's concerned, but the one thing about this American culture is the matter of the Bible as being the final authority on everything.

interview_snippets.speaker: C. T. Vivian
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling the Nashville decision to continue the Freedom Rides after the buses were attacked in 1961, Vivian states the principle he says became the through-line of the movement:
interview_snippets.quote: You see what I mean? We stated that, but that was very, very important because it became the real statement of not only that movement but it became the statement for the rest of our history, when in no way would we allow nonviolence to be destroyed by violence, or violence to destroy nonviolence, right?

interview_snippets.speaker: C. T. Vivian
interview_snippets.lead_in: Held at Parchman Penitentiary in Mississippi after his Freedom Ride arrest, Vivian recounts the moment a guard drew a gun behind his head:
interview_snippets.quote: Oh, no, it's been a large part of my understanding of my identity, right? But the major one for me is that--internally is the one where I got feel that down the back of my head, right, and that this guy wanted to shoot me and didn't, all right? Now, that to me is the deepest piece. It was as though I was warned--I'm walking away from the guy. Parchman is a place where they kill anybody black any time they want to, right? We were the only two there because whatever the prisoners said wouldn't have made a difference, but we couldn't even see them.

interview_snippets.speaker: C. T. Vivian
interview_snippets.lead_in: Describing the February 1965 march to the Dallas County courthouse in Selma where he confronted Sheriff Jim Clark, Vivian says he had not planned the exchange in advance:
interview_snippets.quote: I hadn't even thought up anything. In fact, I had no idea what was going to happen when I got there. I thought they would let us in and we would have some sort of encounter or conversation or whatever with whoever was in charge of the voting.

sources.title: C. T. Vivian oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: C. T. Vivian, Black Power and the American Myth
